Skip to summary
Demo content from a fixture — not a live research run.
~3 min read

Latest AI models

Most leading AI labs now ship three sizes of model per family, a fast small one, a balanced mid one, and a deep flagship that can think before it answers.

2026-05-295 sourcesbeginner
1See the three-tier shape every major lab now ships.2Learn the jargon: tokens, context window, reasoning, multimodal.3Compare the named flagships head-to-head on a few real tasks.4Spot common myths about benchmarks and pricing.5Pick a model for your next project using a short checklist.

Why it matters

The short version

1

Picking the wrong tier wastes money or breaks latency budgets.

2

Capabilities differ more than names suggest, especially on reasoning.

3

Public benchmarks lag real life; a methodology is part of the score.

Concept map

How it fits together

Key ideas

The core concepts

Every family ships three tiers

Labs offer a small fast tier, a mid balanced tier, and a flagship for hard problems. Picking the tier matters more than picking the lab.

Reasoning is its own product surface

Some models can think before answering. They cost more and take longer, but they reduce silly mistakes on math, code, and planning.

Context windows are getting absurd

Modern flagships can read hundreds of thousands of tokens. Bigger is not always better; retrieval still beats stuffing a whole codebase.

Multimodal is default, not optional

Vision is built in across the major families. Audio in and out is shipping fast. You can hand a model a photo or a PDF and ask questions.

Price falls, capability rises

Inference cost per million tokens dropped roughly an order of magnitude in two years. The cheap tier today beats yesterday's flagship.

Timeline

How we got here

2024-05

Flagship sizes consolidate

Labs settle on a small/mid/large shape per family, dropping older one-size-fits-all lines.

2024-09

Reasoning models go mainstream

A dedicated reasoning surface ships across two major labs, with separate pricing and latency profiles.

2025-03

Context jumps past 1M tokens

Long-context flagships read hundreds of thousands of tokens in one call; agentic use cases get viable.

2025-11

Per-token price crosses a cent floor

Mid-tier inference falls below one cent per thousand tokens for many providers.

Comparison

Side by side

What each tier is for

Small

Tier
Small
Strengths
Cheap and fast. Great for routing, classification, short answers.
Watch out for
Drops accuracy on multi-step reasoning.

Mid

Tier
Mid
Strengths
Balanced. The right default for most product features.
Watch out for
Slower than small; can still hallucinate on niche facts.

Flagship

Tier
Flagship
Strengths
Long context, reasoning, planning, multimodal depth.
Watch out for
5-20x cost; latency in seconds; rate limits.
TierStrengthsWatch out for
SmallSmallCheap and fast. Great for routing, classification, short answers.Drops accuracy on multi-step reasoning.
MidMidBalanced. The right default for most product features.Slower than small; can still hallucinate on niche facts.
FlagshipFlagshipLong context, reasoning, planning, multimodal depth.5-20x cost; latency in seconds; rate limits.

Make it concrete

Examples & analogies

Example

Tag 10k customer emails by intent

Use a small-tier model. Batch them. You want throughput, not deep reasoning.

Example

Review a 3-file pull request

Use a mid-tier model with structured output. Ask for findings keyed by file and severity.

Example

Summarize a 200-page contract

Use a flagship with long context. Add retrieval over the table of contents for citations.

Analogy

Picking a model tier is like picking a kitchen knife.

A paring knife is fast and cheap for small jobs. A chef knife is the daily default. A cleaver is overkill until you need to break down a roast.

Analogy

Reasoning vs non-reasoning is like driving vs taking a long way with a map.

Driving fast is fine when the route is obvious. The detour with a map is slower but stops you missing the exit.

Myth vs truth

Clear up the confusion

Myth

Bigger model = better answer.

Truth

On most product tasks the mid tier wins on cost-quality. Use the flagship only when you measured a real gap.

Myth

Benchmark leaderboards tell you which model to ship.

Truth

Public benchmarks leak into training data and rarely match your eval. Run your own evals on your own tasks.

Myth

A bigger context window solves retrieval.

Truth

Models miss details in the middle of long contexts. Retrieval and chunking still help, even at 1M tokens.

Glossary

Plain-English definitions

token

A chunk of text the model reads or writes. Roughly four characters of English.

context window

The maximum tokens the model can see in one call, prompt plus answer.

reasoning model

A model trained to plan and verify steps before answering. Slower, more accurate on hard tasks.

multimodal

A model that takes more than one input type, like text and images, or text and audio.

flagship

The most capable model in a family. Highest cost, latency, and quality ceiling.

benchmark

A standardized test used to compare models. Useful for ranking, weak for product decisions.

retrieval

Pulling only the relevant chunks of a document into the prompt instead of stuffing everything.

Takeaways

What to do with this

Default to the mid tier; promote to flagship only after a real eval shows a gap.

Cache prompts and structured outputs; cost scales with what you re-send.

Pair retrieval with long context; do not throw whole codebases at the model.

Run a small private eval before you swap providers; benchmarks lie about your tasks.

Re-price every quarter; per-token cost has dropped about an order of magnitude in two years.

Sources

Where this comes from

What we're unsure about

Specific model names and prices change monthly; this fixture intentionally avoids naming exact versions.

Context-window numbers vary by provider and tier; treat the 1M figure as illustrative, not a contract.

Generated by Recap Studio. One page, offline, yours. Be skeptical — check the sources.
